comments.title: Sweat lodge/sauna
comments.content: Hello Mr. Greer. I am interested in using a sauna on a regular basis for my physical health.  I know that many native Americans used sweat lodges for spiritual reasons. Do you know if performing magic, divination, meditation, prayer, or any other occult practices are enhanced when done under these circumstances?  Are there any drawbacks?

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content: For some reason -- I'm not sure whether anybody knows why -- the use of saunas and heat/steam baths of the same sort never spread to western Europe. That's where the magical traditions I've studied come from, and so there isn't much information in the Western esoteric tradition about such things. I know that in the many, many cultures that use heat or steam baths, there are plenty of spiritual practices that revolve around them, but the details? I'm sorry to say you'll have to ask someone who has the relevant experience.

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content: At least from my experience of temazcalli --the mesoamerican version of the steam baths-- it would be very hard to focus on meditation, divination or ritual while your body is sweating profusely from every possible pore! The whole thing is ritualistically prepared with layers of symbolism from lighting the fire to the heating of volcanic stones with it to entering the lodge but once you are inside it you just let go, there's not much space for anything else but chanting and talking with the person guiding it or just remaining quiet to see what comes to you. As a simpler version it occurs to me you could pray to the divine entities you associate with fire and water as a thanks and for guidance but I wouldn't try to exert myself unnecessarily inside of it, at least in temazcallis people have passed out just by not being careful enough with timing and hydration.

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content:
The use of the bathhouse or sauna for magic is very much a Northern European thing, still alive among the northern Scandinavians and the East Slavs (Russians, Ukrainians, Belarusians), but it is not used in the same way as among Native Americans.

The Russian version of the sauna gives the title for William F. Ryan's superb book on Russian magic, The Bathhouse at Midnight: An Historical Survey of Magic and Divination in Russia. Ryan is a first-rate scholar (and not, I think, a practitioner). He was the librarian at the Warburg Institute in London for 26 years, which is a higher honor than many academic chairs.

A traditional Russian peasant village is a small cosmos of concentric circles. It has a Russian Orthodox church at its center, surrounded by homes with their yards, gardens and outbuildings. The next circle, moving outwards, is the village's fields; and the next circle after that is the forest, where only the hunters and foragers ever dare to go. Each circle has its characteristic sets of spirits: house-spirits, barn-spirits, field-spirits, forest-spirits, and so forth. As you move outwards, each set of spirits you encounter is less and less sympathetic to humans, and the spirits of the deep forest can be positively dangerous to encounter. There is a whole world of these spirits in all their many kinds (each with its own proper name in Russian).

There are two buildings that should be placed outside the village for mundane reasons, and thus they offend against this cosmic pattern.

One is the water-mill, which has to be situated on a river. This is placed outside the village because it is a dangerous building in a purely mundane sense: if the miller is careless or drunk, the mill will explode and level part of the village. (Finely ground flour, when it gets too hot, will explode like gunpowder. So the miller has to control how much heat the grindstones create by their friction as they mill the flour. He does this by smell: he has to "keep his nose to the grindstone," literally.) The mill-race is also dangerous, because one can easily drown in it if one slips or falls into it carelessly. The spirits of the mill(-race) are therefore thought to demand human sacrifice from time to time, and the miller, who appears to have overmastered them, is credited with uncanny power and regarded as a dangerous man to cross.

The other building placed outside the village is the bathhouse or sauna. This is dangerous for other reasons, partly mundane, partly spiritual. Its mundane dangers arise from the fire which heats it, and which must be carefully watched: it may set the building on fire, or deplete the oxygen inside to a lethal level.

The spiritual danger of the bathhouse comes from the people using it, who have shed their clothes to wash themselves. Some dare-devils among the bathers may even go so far as to take off the small metal crosses which all Russian Othodox people traditionally wear around their necks to ward off the small devils (бесики) that always swarm around humans like flies around carrion.

And this is what can make the bathhouse a place of great danger: it is also the isolated place where one will go at midnight to work the most powerful magic, magic that needs the help of these small devils, or greater spirits than them, to work. One gets naked, sheds one's protective cross, and calls on the appropriate spirits to work one's spells.

So, at least in Russia, the use of the bathhouse or sauna for magic is different from the use of the sweat-lodge by Native Americans.

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content:
Anything that I've read from the Finnish/Scandinavian tradition (which is not a lot) concentrates on their use for purification and healing. There's also a bit about keeping good relations with the sauna spirit, which is treated in much the same way as nisse and given porridge on Yule. In contrast with the Russian tradition, women used to give birth in the sauna, as it was both spiritually and physically clean.

--Sister Crow

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content:
Thank you so much this, I will look for this book.

MILLICENTLY LURKING

replies.title: Re: Sweat lodge/sauna
replies.content:
I take sauna regularly so can share some thoughts.

The traditional practice in Michigan’s UP (where sauna culture originally came from Finland I believe) is to cool off in a lake or snow bank between stints in the sauna. That alteration of extreme hot and cold creates a clear, slightly euphoric state of mind that is very conducive to meditation.

From an occult point of view, the sauna strongly incorporates all of the four elements. Fire heats stones, water on stones creates steam. The result is a very primal experience where you feel the steam, listen to the fire, the dripping and hissing of water, feel the heat radiating from the rocks.

I’m not aware of drawbacks, as long as one is careful not to overheat. Or touch the hot iron sauna stove 😁

comments.title: Thoughts on "Way of the Rose" and How to Make Tangible Intellectual Insights
comments.content:
Good Evening,

A happy (late) Groundhog Day to all! May you be blessed with a pleasant spring sooner rather than later.

To Share: After an anonymous commenter here spoke well of The Way of the Rose by Clark Strand and Perdita Finn, I added it to my Rosary reading list, and once my year of reading books by the dead was wrapped up, I jumped on it right away. I've written up my thoughts on the book here: https://jpowellrussell.com/#thoughts_on_the_way_of_the_rose.

tl;dr version: Interesting and insightful book on the Rosary from a non-Catholic, and not even necessarily Christian, standpoint, that nonetheless takes it seriously spiritually. I had a few quibbles with the feminist and perennialist-ish outlooks in the book, but overall, I think it's very helpful for anyone wanting to know more about the Rosary, but for whom the traditional Catholic approach doesn't work for one reason or another, including, as in my case, those who want to use the Rosary as a model for some very different prayers..

To Ask: My journaling, meditation, and so forth have suggested to me that I might be too afraid of getting hurt. Normally, if I have an intellectual realization about myself, the next step to make it more real is to "practice" it, but I'm a bit stumped here - I don't want to seek out getting "hurt" (whether physically, emotionally, or otherwise) on purpose, as that doesn't seem all that healthy. I'm already working with an affirmation to be braver, but any further thoughts on how to handle taking an intellectual insight like this and putting it into practice when the obvious way to "practice" is harmful?

As always, thanks very much to JMG and everyone else here.

To any who will have them, I put forth my blessings and best wishes,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on "Way of the Rose" and How to Make Tangible Intellectual Insights
replies.content: Hmm. Your life may be more blissful than most; I've never had to wait long for some unavoidable "opportunity," if you want to call it that, to experience some form of pain. You might see if there are situations where avoiding pain costs you more than the pain would, and go ahead and accept the pain in order to achieve something else of more importance.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on "Way of the Rose" and How to Make Tangible Intellectual Insights
replies.content: Thank you, that's a helpful framing. Being receptive to what the world has to show me, rather than going out and finding things/making things happen, is another area I'm working on, so I'm not surprised I'd look right past that obvious approach.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on "Way of the Rose" and How to Make Tangible Intellectual Insights
replies.content: I have found it fruitful to consider every problematic human behavior as an attempt to avoid experiencing pain. Revenge, for instance; the satisfaction one experiences in an act of revenge, I have thought, simply is the maintenance of the feeling that you haven't been hurt by the other's offense. Considering the situation symbolically as a three dimensional object with a smooth flat top and a jagged, complex underside, you are choosing to look at the "flat top," when you take revenge--at the comforting idea, which is that you are still invulnerable, having displaced the hurt you registered unconsciously, the scream to be noticed of which is your anger, onto another person by hurting them. It is a penal substitution. One shifts the threatening pressure of awareness off of one's egoity onto another individual. The jagged underside is that one's own emotional equilibrium was compromised by another's actions. If one makes a negative value judgment about one's vulnerability in such a case, the sense is that one has been humiliated or lessened.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on "Way of the Rose" and How to Make Tangible Intellectual Insights
replies.content:
First thought: getting hurt can happen on multiple levels... you appear to be quite erudite and approach things intellectually. With your military background, perhaps the fear is of being emotionally or spiritually hurt. In some cases where a person is afraid of emotional suffering, they trend toward sympathy or intellectualized empathy for others and end up having problems in relationships. This can also come up as a lack of vulnerability that can lead to imbalances. Perhaps this could also happen on a spiritual level, where deeper faith is blocked by fear of being hurt or ignored by the divine/deity.

Second thought: I know you practice a form of heathenry. Perhaps strike up a conversation with Tyr. I find his presence in the stories to be a very steady one, and his sacrificial act to be one not only of courage/bravery but also of emotional vulnerability because worse than sacrificing his hand, he is sacrificing the trust of Fenrir, whom he is shown having an affectionate, near parental, relationship with.

Wishing you wellness on your journey.

replies.content:

comments.title: Qs
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

Some minor questions today:

1) Would divining if I should do
a) modern order of essenes lessons be L9?
b) if I should do tibetan 5 rites be L9 or 6?
c) similarly, the old order of essenes course work for emotions, or the order of spiritual alchemy courses be L9?

2) Is there any thing to be done to improve memory (besides memory palaces and spaces repetition)? Does mercury help with memory at all?

Similar to what you said last week, I suspect I have a neurological issue with my physical or etheric body that causes recall of memories to be an issue (even though I a very good at picking things up), which is completely out my control.

3) You suggested the protective amulet last week against evil eyes, I will look into it; would Sphere of protection or one of the other rituals also be effective against particular negative astral influences sent my way?

3) Is it generally considered okay or rude to have a mixed altar of deities across pantheons?

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
1) I'd treat all of them but the Five Rites as 9th house issues -- the Rites are 6th house -- but I'd check the other house and see what figure was in it, just to be sure.

2) Saturn is the planet of memory. He never forgets anything, and you can invoke him to improve your memory. One caution -- if you do this, you will have to remember things you might prefer to forget!

3) Yes, very much so. A daily banishing ritual of some kind -- Sphere of Protection, Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, or Judson exercise -- is the form of protective work I most recommend; it's just that many people aren't willing to commit to daily ritual.

4) No, in polytheist societies it's perfectly normal. In Roman times, for example, the Celtic goddess Epona was adopted as the patron deity of the Roman cavalry, so that you find altars to her all over the Roman world, sometimes in temples to other deities. In the same way, plenty of Romans who prayed to Jove and the other Roman gods also made offerings to Isis, Mithras, Cybele, and other non-Roman deities without feeling the least incongruity. The idea that everything has to be divided up strictly by pantheon is a modern notion, and rather a silly one all things considered -- it's not as though the deities get bent out of shape about human cultural boundaries, after all!

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
Thank you very much.

On 2, oh that surprises me, I always figured mercury would hold domain over memory. With saturn, a positive divination to planetary charity is probably the right idea. What type of charity do you think would be appropriate?

On 3, I've been reluctant to do ritual work because of both a weak L8 (which made me sort of wary of ritual work generally) and my work life being extreme busy at many points (enthroned L10), but there's enough negative attention professionally that I feel like I should do something besides prayer daily. I'm going divine which of those 3 would be appropriate, but any if you have any thoughts on which one to pick, happy to hear them.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:
2) Nah, Mercury's too flighty to be good at memory. As for Saturn, any charity having to do with old people, the poor, or ancient things is suitable -- for example, you can make a donation to a fund that preserves an archeological site.

3) It's really a personal matter. Read through the descriptions, consider trying all three, and see which one feels best to you.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content: 4) One of my favorite historical examples is Emperor Severus Alexander's personal altar, which included, in addition to his traditional deities, statues of Christ and Abraham.  I sometimes wonder if the modern hang-ups about mixing pantheons isn't just a holdover from monotheistic thought. At least the classical Pagans didn't seem all that put off by it.

replies.title: Re: Qs
replies.content:

comments.title: Seeking daily rituals that can be done in car

I have been putting off starting a daily practice because I have no privacy where I live and it's something I insist on doing privately. I am working on changing my arrangements, but I no longer accept this as an excuse to not start.

It occurred to me that I am alone in my car for a little while every single day, and I almost always take some time to just stop somewhere and chill for a bit or read a bit.

Is there a simple banishing or even a more complete set of daily rituals I can do seated in the car? Or sometimes on a park bench, but very inconspicuously? I am not for or against any particular religions or ideologies. I think for now it's mostly about forming the habit, but also about beginning to build some spiritual protection for myself.

(Sorry... such a simple question need not be so wordy, but there you are) :)

Many thanks (for all you do, and for the presence of this wonderful group).

I have a crippling fear of loneliness and aging alone (although I'm not even 30). I particularly fear my husband dying and find myself ruminating on my wish to never live a single day without him. I'd honestly rather die tomorrow. Our relationship is deep and irreplaceable and I struggle to connect with others
(autistic). Any tips on learning to live better with this fear? Also, any tips on learning to live with the fear of dying (not of death but of the dying process)?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, this is probably something left over from a previous life, and should be dealt with constructively before it messes up this life. One approach that works well for many people is journaling. Put in 15-30 minutes a day writing about your fears, having dialogues with them, and unpacking them so that the old emotions have a chance to vent instead of staying clogged inside you.

You might also find the practices of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy useful -- you can find their preliminary lessons available for free download here. The OSA work focuses on unpacking burdensome emotions from the past, releasing them, and learning to live more freely.

But I wasn't sure if I was making it all up because the fear is so relevant to me now. Never the less I do have a sense that I am supposed to heal these fears in this life - could that be true?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Yes, that could indeed be true. It quite often happens that the seemingly unreasonable emotional reactions of this life are based on things that actually happened in the last life. For example, I've always disliked riding in cars and never got a driver's license; when I started to recover past life memories, one of the things I remembered first was the details of the car crash that ended my last life. (After I recovered those memories, I considered learning to drive, and decided against it for financial and environmental reasons -- but I could have chosen to do so.)

comments.title: Pathworking
comments.content:
Hi JMG, I have recently finished my third stage meditations on the Tree of Life in Paths of Wisdom. I’ve begun pathworking in earnest since then and I have a few questions.
I’m not sure how much “control” I should be trying to exert over the process. I usually have to consciously get the imagery going before it kicks in and takes on its own life, so to speak. Should I try to keep the initial phases of the path consistent, or allow the imagery to be radically different if it seems to be moving that way?
Your guidance in the book mentions slowing down and looking for details, which is useful because my pathworkings seem to have an inherent momentum to them. Sometimes the details move and shift, and when I try to read it is like trying to read in a dream! Is this just astral static?
Also, I sometimes have flashes during my meditations on the pathworking that include symbols or details that I missed during the initial pathworking. At least once so far, the meditation has taken on a bit of the quality of a pathworking (an entity that wasn’t present during the pathworking made itself known). Is it better to follow up on those events during the meditation when they arise, or let them pass and try to explore them during the next pathworking? Is meditation a good time to try to “talk” to entities that I was unable to interact with during the pathworking due to the momentum I mentioned earlier?

Thank you for any guidance you can provide.

replies.title: Re: Pathworking
replies.content:
1) Try to keep the initial phases consistent. After that, let it go where it wants to go.

2) Yes, it's astral static. Once you get an image, it can sometimes be necessary to hold it in place consciously for a little while, in order for the rest of what it has to communicate to come through the astral noise.

3) In my experience, at least, it's best to deal with such things as they arise. If you find yourself having a conversation with an entity, have the conversation, then write down all the details and meditate on those later!

replies.title: Re: Pathworking
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: The Divine

replies.title: Re: The Divine
replies.content:
When someone says just "the Divine," I think of something like the Tao: the foundation of reality, or Being Itself in a more modern parlance. As such, its love is not a form of personal affection. Love at its root is willing what is good for someone or something, and the Divine wills our good by setting up the universe so we can work out our karma, learning our lessons and developing our characters.

This view is compatible with polytheism. Monotheism is identifying one particular deity with the Divine as such, especially when you believe it to be literally true. (If you make that identification for the purposes of spiritual practice without believing it is literally the case, that's a form of henotheism instead of strict monotheism.)

replies.title: Re: The Divine
replies.content:
John's use of the term "the Divine" was very carefully chosen. Remember that he taught people from many different backgrounds, including a good many Native American students; he needed a way of talking about deity that would make room for medicine spirits, Pagan deities, and the Christian god, among others. If pressed, he would say that it's not helpful to be more precise; the OSA material (and, for that matter, the Modern Order of Essenes material) assumes only that there is present in the cosmos we inhabit at least one transcendent power that radiates unconditional love.

To a Christian, that's Christ; to a Pagan, that's one or more deities; to a Buddhist, well, there's a long line of Buddhas, bodhisattvas, arhats, et al. to choose from; to believers in traditional Native American faiths, exactly what it is depends from one tribal tradition to another, and so on. If you're not a believer in any particular faith, all John would ask is that you accept the possibility that there is such a power in the cosmos, and that you can come into contact with it.

replies.title: Re: Emblem 32

This is more a question about Paganism than Occultism, so feel free to delete and I’ll be happy to ask on the monthly open post instead.

Knowledge of the Norse, Greek and Roman gods is fairly widespread today, even in popular culture. Maybe a dumb question, but how come we never hear about the pantheons of the rest of pre-Christian Europe?

Were those two really the only two pantheons, and places in the middle of Europe just borrowed from both as they pleased? replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, but this is mostly due to what got written down by whom and when.

Super short version: the Greeks and the Romans wrote down a lot of stuff about their Gods when they still believed in Them and worshipped Them. Despite converting to Christianity, many of these writings were still regarded as worthwhile and/or left in libraries and forgotten, to be dusted off and given more prominence in the Classical-loving Renaissance and Early modern period.

With the Norse Gods, the Nordic countries were among the last countries of Western Europe to convert to Christianity (unlike Anglo-Saxon England, which was fairly early), and often somewhat unevenly (Christian kings, Heathen peasants, basically), so you had recent belief and worship once the Nordic countries became more widely literate under Christianity. But the real reason we know so much about the Norse myths is because one guy, Snorri Sturlusson, loved the poetic traditions of his people (the Icelanders) and realized that you literally couldn't write traditional poetry without understanding the myths, because they are chock-full of stock metaphors called "kennings", like "Freyja's Tears" or "Otter's Ransom" to mean gold. So he wrote the Prose Edda, which summarized what he knew about the Gods and their mythology, which allowed the poems of the Poetic Edda (a collection of the kind of traditional poems Snorri wanted to be able to keep emulating) to be much better understood.

My understanding is that in the rest of Europe, for one reason or another, the cultures didn't have a tradition of writing down their myths before Christianization (whether due to a taboo on writing them down, as among the Druids, or because literacy wasn't used for much of anything, as in some eastern European countries). So, for these places, we have to hunt for hints and scraps in Christianized accounts (like Beowulf or the Mabinogion) and match them with what we can figure out from archaeology and comparative mythology.

On the Anglo-Saxon Gods, specifically, I'm of the view that it seems to have been very close to the Norse one, more of a regional variation than utterly different sets of Gods. My very favorite discussion of what we can know/guess about pre-Christian Anglo-Saxon belief is The Elder Gods by Stephen Pollington.

If you'd like to get into that more, let me know, as it's a particular area of interest for me.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I've been studying pre-Christian religions most of my adult life, and the conclusion I've come to is that if it wasn't written down, it's pretty much lost. We have very little in the way of written sources from the Celts, Anglo-Saxons, Slavs, Normans, and all the other tribal groupings that rose and fell throughout Europe pre-Christianity.

In some cases, all we have is a single god name from a single votive statue in the Black Forest of Germany, for instance, and what that name might even means comes down to guess work by linguists. Honestly, we know more about ancient Mesopotamian religion because they pressed it into clay tablets, then we do about Iron Age Celtic religion. We can make some educated guesses and pull connections together from various sources, but it's all very intangible. We know the historical Druids had a strong oral tradition that must have been filled with knowledge and wisdom passed down through many many generations, but that was lost by the Roman persecutions.

I also think it has to do with population size. Much of northern and western Europe was grouped into, by modern standards at least, smaller tribes than many of our modern towns of today. They didn't have the need, or resources, for specializations like the scribes of Egypt or Mesopotamia or the sprawling urbanity of Roman cities.

There also really wasn't necessarily pantheons such as in Greece and Rome. Generally you had a tribal god who looked out for your tribal grouping specifically that was likely generally all purpose, and gods of place and natural phenomena - springs, mountains, forests, crop and livestock fertility, rain and thunder, maybe some clan heroes, and then there was the ancestor cults. I'm painting it in broad strokes, of course, but those are my general thoughts on the matter.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's purely a matter of what got written down and then didn't get burnt. We know next to nothing about the Anglo-Saxon gods because so few documents survived. There were at least a dozen epics about the doings of heroes in and around the court of Hrothgar, king of the Shield-Danes; the only one that survives is Beowulf, and only one copy of that happened to reach modern times intact. The same sort of losses were suffered by most of the other Pagan traditions of old Europe. The Greek and Roman myths survived in literary form, and the Norse myths survived in isolated Iceland in the form of the Eddas; some elements of Irish myth got included in compilations of the Christian era, such as the Book of Invasions; but nearly everything else has been lost, utterly and forever.

replies.content:
I used to be really obsessed with psychedelics because of the works of Alexander and Ann Shulgin, who dedicated their lives to testing hundreds of novel drugs on themselves. Their books PiHKAL and TiHKAL are worth reading if only because they are utterly unique pieces of literature. One might note she died just last year at the ripe age of 91, and he died in 2014 at age 88. He did have vascular dementia at the end of his life, but plenty of people who have never done drugs develop cognitive problems.

I stopped taking them owing to, out of perhaps fifty trips, two embarrassing incidents as a result of accidental overdoses. Since the second one I have never really had the stomach for it, and the magical practitioners I've known who take them either talk as if they don't practice daily or struggle. I know one successful occultist who used to combine and doesn't anymore. When I asked him if he found that those combinations left behind some mess that had to be gotten out of, he said, "Oh, yeah!" A teacher of mine who's also experimented says that people with natal Saturn-Neptune squares should steer clear.

I had lots of fascinating experiences, but the extreme phenomenal states I would enter were never entirely comfortable. I'd often feel a lot more lively and out of my shell for two weeks-to a month after taking a strong dose of long-acting psychedelic. But the feeling fades, and you have to go back again.

As far as long-term effects, those two "incidents" left me feeling like myself again within several hours, just very shaken.

replies.title: Re: Capoeira
replies.content:
A few thoughts on the matter, the most immediate being that you should be very careful in mixing this with Druidry, some divination would be wise.

1) If you are serious about fighting, this art is a terrible choice. The Capoeristas I've met have been unusually delusional about their ability. The only competent martial artist I know that does Capoeira is pretty open about the fact that he thinks it is tremendous fun, not for fighting.

2) I'm not convinced that it is American at all. My Brazillian friends all consider it an explicitly African practice.

3) The ritualistic elements are explicitly drawn from african diaspora religions. A tamanous connection thus seems improbable.

4) Dance and body movements are excellent vehicles for etheric work. For comparison, a great deal of the etheric work in Taijiquan looks like nothing more than unusually precise muscle control.

Whatever you decide to do, I wish you luck. The martial arts have been a tremendous positive impact on my own life. But it sounds like you are walking towards accidentally mixing spiritual traditions.

comments.title: Ancestors
comments.content:
Hi,

This kind of goes along with a question that was asked recently about the trend of people insisting that you need to practice magic in line with your heritage. Another trend I've noticed is people insisting that you need to do some kind of work with your ancestors. This seems to have become emphasized as the ATRs have become more popular (Ifa, Vodou, etc.) which place a heavy emphasis on this. I'm seeing people get really dogmatic about this, saying that you won't progress unless you work with your ancestors.

I did some ancestor practices in the past, and to be honest, I didn't feel I got a lot out of it. Most of my ancestors on both sides were some variety of Catholic and would frown on my practices now. And as a believer in reincarnation, I tend to think my older ancestors have long since either reincarnated or moved on if they had some spiritual attainment. I frankly feel closer to a friend who passed a few years ago than some dead relatives (including a dead parent).

Do ancestors really have much to do with magical or spiritual attainment?

replies.title: Re: Ancestors
replies.content:
Some traditions do ancestor work; others don't. Any time people from one tradition insist that something they do is absolutely essential for everyone else, they're demonstrating their ignorance of the diversity of human spiritual traditions. I quite agree that if you practice an African traditional religion, and your teacher says you should work with your ancestors, then you had better hop to it -- but if you don't practice an ATR, and the tradition you practice doesn't include ancestor work, then that advice does not apply.

I don't do ancestor work. I've never felt that it was appropriate to my personal spiritual path. I don't object to people doing it if they feel it's appropriate, or if they follow a path that requires it, but I don't and I don't. So you can make up your own mind, and let the dogmatists keep barking up their respective trees.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings JMG,

I've been meditating on the connection between the numbers 1 through 10 and our thought process, following some inspiration from LRM. It seems that the way our rational mind functions can be grouped in either a unitary, binary, or ternary mode. In the unitary mode, we hold an object of thought unthinkingly and implicitly, without self-reflection and without being aware of any alternative or opposition. This is often the case before we've had the chance to examine an issue with conscious deliberation. In the binary mode, we're aware of an object of thought and its opposite; binary thinking often involves a value judgement, where one alternative is 'good' and its opposite is 'bad'. In ternary thinking, we're able to stand above the binary and examine both options from a detached perspective, seeing the advantages and disadvantages, strengths and weaknesses, merits and demerits, of both objects of thought, and attaining a golden mean which is a compromise of both.

A preliminary conclusion that seem to be suggested by the above:

- As we increase our mode of thought, corresponding to higher numbers, the nuance of our thinking increases. Unitary thinking is pre-conscious, binary thinking is often simplistic and overly rigid, ternary thinking is superior to both, and so on.

However, I'm struggling to take this analogy beyond the number 3. What exactly does 'quaternary' thinking (and beyond) look like? What are some examples of it that aren't too artificial?

replies.content: It's very difficult to think beyond the number 3! There are few examples because very few people do it. I'd say, though, that each number has its value as a tool for thinking. There are times when unitary or binary thinking are appropriate; you just need not to get stuck in them.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I have yet to come across a historically long-lasting system of spiritual practice that aims at permanent transformation of conciousness that doesn't at least implicitly insist that some form of sexual asceticism is necessary, whether it be celibacy or having sex only with one's opposite-sex spouse.

Even in left-hand Hindu tantra, it seems that what is unsaid is that you only break taboos during ritual. The predominant contemporary view in alternative spirituality seems to be that whatever kind or amount of sex you have, as long as you are not violating anyone's boundaries, doesn't have any effect on whether you get enlightened. And it may be reasonable to ignore the myriad, highly specific schemes of sexual ethics proffered by the world's religions and esoteric systems, as it seems no one is capable of being objective about how to approach sex, sleep, physical safety, or eating. With sex people freak out over what's OK, same with how much and what kind of sleep you get, same with protecting yourself, and same with diet. People, myself included, just can't seem to leave activity in those areas of life to instinct; yet, as someone who spent years eating special diets, it seems to free up willpower to strive for excellence in pursuits that go beyond instinct--e.g., intellectual--when you leave instincts to, uhh, instinct.

Nonetheless, those who combine Hermeticism in particular, which has a long history of discomfort with instinct, with libertinism, seem to get meaningful, but tortuous, results. I am thinking of Aleister Crowley, who, though I find his spirituality aesthetically unappealing, was a far more illustrious individual than I. Critiquing him is above my pay grade.

Are the unpredictable results just the result of trying to operate as a libertine within egregores that exclude libertinism? Or is leaving instinct to instinct actually incompatible with higher echela of spiritual development? Perhaps leaving instinct to instinct is just incompatible with the complex societies in which widely-known religions and esoteric systems sprung up, and the values of which they mirror and reinforce. Perhaps you'd have to look to a paganism that's so pagan, the word has its original connotation of "bumpkin" or "uncivilized." In that case, the intellectual paganism from which the emanationist cosmology that seems to be the backbone of ceremonial magic emerged is right out. Or do you have to be somewhat ascetic to get good results from ceremonial magic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Good heavens, no. The idea that spiritual development always requires asceticism is inaccurate, though it's a widely held belief. In traditional Jewish Cabalistic circles, for example, you must be married in order to receive anything but the most basic instruction, and in some cases the unmarried don't even get that. The mystically inclined end of Shinto in Japan isn't quite so strict, but it's normal for practitioners to be married and have an ordinary sex life. I'm also reminded that the Portuguese merchants who were the first Westerners to encounter Japanese culture up close were shocked to discover that most Zen practitioners at that time were gay.

The crucial point is that your sex life, and every other instinctual part of your life -- for example, eating and sleeping -- needs to be balanced and moderate, so it doesn't interfere with your practices and the development of higher states of consciousness. If you're constantly panting after sex, that's going to distract you, but the same is true if you're an obsessive foodie, or for that matter if you've got an obsessive concern with your bowel movements. Balance, moderation, and stability are essential if you're going to seek the heights of human consciousness.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
JMG,

(1) I rather like the Thoth tarot deck, but I’m slightly concerned Crowley’s influence makes it unbalanced and somehow dangerous. Is there any reason to be chary of that deck and its egregore?

(2) Do you find anything of merit in the Law of Thelema?

Thank you as always for your wonderful forum and for generously sharing your time with us knuckleheads.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) The Thoth deck is very blunt -- if you're being an idiot it will tell you that in no uncertain terms -- but I've used it with good results. I don't know of any reason not to use it if you find it appealing.

2) That's a challenging question, for two reasons.

First, I'm convinced that Crowley completely misunderstood what it meant (and means) to speak the Word of an Aeon. An aeon isn't a period of time; as the Gnostics understood, it's an eternal spiritual reality. (They counted thirty of them.) Every aeon is always present and always an option, and each magus -- each serious magical practitioner -- speaks the Word or essential logos of whatever aeon is the root of his or her higher self. Thus Crowley spoke the word of one aeon -- in the Valentinian Gnosis, this was the Aeon Autophyês, the Self-Existent, the aeon of freedom and eternal independence -- because that was the aeon from which his soul had its origin. That didn't make him the One True Prophet of the Age, because every magus has the same right and the same inescapable necessity to speak his or her own Word. So Crowley was expressing one of thirty or so basic energies of the cosmos, and his Law is only relevant to those who are like him descended from that aeon, and agree with his idiosyncratic expression of that aeon's energy.

Second, next to nobody seems to pay attention to the verb tenses in Crowley's formulation of his Law: "Do what thou wilt shall be the whole of the Law; Love is the Law, Love under Will." To say that "Do what thou wilt" shall be the whole of the law -- notice the future tense! -- implies that it isn't the whole of the law now, only that at some unspecified point in the future it will be. Here and now, by contrast, love under will is the law: that's what it claims. That's not at all the way most Thelemites I've encountered seem to interpret it!

All of that is necessary background for my answer. For those who feel called to the same position Crowley took, it's quite possible that the Law of Thelema is relevant. It's not relevant to the other 29/30th of humanity, and that includes me. Love under will is not my law, and for me, it has no merit. But of course your mileage may vary!

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Sorry for the late question (I was baking a whole lotta bread using your Weird of Hali book and time got away from me). I've been considering emmigrating to Britain or possibly some other nearby western Euro country to start a new life and set down new roots. I had received some possible synchronicities in response to a request to my gods for an omen of their advice for me, so I ran the following geomancy divinations:

"Do you [deity] think I should move to Britain?"
https://amissio.net/gen/tradshield/be/10.svg

I keep telling myself not to overthink things, but the bad witnesses in combination with the Lesser Fortune, the figure of speedy yet inconstant success, seems like an unfortunate figure for my question. The way of the points also leads to the 4th daughter, which does indeed show the driving force behind my question (I'm looking for true love). So I ran another divination:

"Will I find the true love I am seeking in Britain?"
https://amissio.net/gen/tradshield/bc/94.svg

Again with the unfortunate left witness, and the exact same judge! Clearly it's trying to tell me something, but what? The way of the points leads again to the 4th daughter, but the bodily and character description of the figure in it is the mirror opposite of what I'm looking for, and a bad figure for starting a relationship anyways.

I could read these charts two ways:
a) Yes, quick success proceeding from outside of you (Fortuna Minor) is assured. It will be an end (Cauda Draconis) to your lonely chastity (Albus) and when you find love you will buy some property and settle down (Carcer).
b) Yes, you'll find a girl, but it won't last (Fortuna Minor) and you'll end up feeling miserable and isolated (Carcer).

When you have trouble deciding between the dual meanings of a figure, how do you resolve it (I tried using the reconciler, same dual meaning problem of Tristitia potentially meaning putting down roots or great sorrow)?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: You're overthinking it. You asked a yes/no question in both cases, and the answer in both cases is "no." In the first reading, two bad witnesses combined with the very ambiguous Fortuna Minor amounts to "DON'T DO IT!"  In the second case, yes, you'll meet someone, but it won't work out and you'll end up miserable. Always, always, when you're asking a yes/no question, use the favorable or unfavorable meanings of the figures as the key to the answer.
